data.title: Apple: The Future Is More Privacy And Security In AI
data.description:
I rate Apple (NASDAQ:AAPL) as a buy, as there are big opportunities to further develop new businesses with the record of 2.35 billion active devices worldwide achieved in 2025. Even when some pundits say that Apple is behind in AI compared to its M7 counterparts, there are key factors that they are overlooking with respect to Apple's AI requirements, which are more complex in many ways than those of its counterparts, among other things, because of the privacy and security of the user's data, which is a very important pillar in Apple's long-term strategy, as mentioned in my last article in June 2024. That is not currently so much appreciated, but it will be over the long haul. In addition, Apple delayed the rollout of some key features of Apple Intelligence for 2026, which gave some bear analysts and pundits some arguments in their favor to be a bear of Apple stock; again, that is a mistake since Apple is following a completely different strategy compared to the AI advances of its counterparts. Indeed, it's often said that as Apple delays its AI features, its main counterparts are advancing without difficulties, but this argument is flawed as Apple's AI requirements work in a very different way involving privacy, hardware integration, user experience standards, etc. Even though Apple's Siri has limitations right now while not offering all that was promised last year at the last WWDC 2024, Apple offers alternative options to compensate for this delay in the AI features, which I believe guarantees that sales will not be impacted materially in 2025 for this reason. Apple is not rushing its AI developments, but once finished, they will represent a long-term tailwind. Let's see it more deeply.

Context

According to the Q2 2025 results, in the first six months of the fiscal year, Apple grew 4.4% YoY, operating income grew 6.1% YoY, and net income grew 6.2% YoY in the same period. Free cash flow decreased 17% YoY in the first six months of FY 2025 as a result of the significant increase in the current and non-current liabilities, which most likely are the lease arrangements for certain equipment and facilities, including corporate, data center, manufacturing, and retail space.

As we can see in the cash flow statements, that increase reveals that Apple is working to expand its operations and retail stores. In regard to the revenue breakdown, we can see the following:

In the table above, we can see that Apple still depends on the iPhone, which represents 53% of the total sales in the first six months of FY 2025, a percentage not so different from last year's; however, services are increasing their participation in the total sales over the years, representing 24% in the last results for Q2 2025. It's interesting to see how services only represented 15% of total sales in 2018.

In addition, in terms of geography, America still represents the most important market for Apple's revenues, whereas China has reduced its participation in the last years, most likely because of the delay in the AI rollout and competition; however, we can see in the table above that in 2019 and 2020, China represented a lower percentage of the total revenues compared to 2018, but then, there was a recovery in 2021 on. It's important to see things in perspective to avoid the negative noise about the fact that Apple was losing market share in China in the last quarters, which indicates that these short-term trends might be temporary.

On the other hand, Europe and the rest of Asia Pacific have been growing their participation in Apple's total sales in the last few years.

Pundits consider Apple behind in AI, but that's a short-term vision

At first sight, it seems so when we see Siri's limitations compared to other AI assistants such as Google's Gemini, ChatGPT, Amazon's Alexa, and Samsung's Galaxy AI. Siri was acquired by Apple in 2010 with Steve Jobs involved in the acquisition; its artificial intelligence debuted in 2011, being a pioneer for giving users the ability to talk to their smartphone.

Siri's capabilities were made possible using machine learning, which was an advanced development at the time. In 2018, John Giannandrea, who led Search and Artificial Intelligence at Google, was hired to lead Siri, but it turned out this hiring did not change much about Siri's performance over the next years. Recently, in March 2025, there was news that Apple had replaced Giannandrea with Apple's Vision Pro chief, Mark Rockwell.

I think that this move is very interesting, as we already know Rockwell's impressive results with the Vision Pro glasses, showing significant technological advances combined with several potential applications, so under Mark Rockwell's leadership, I could expect better results in Siri's AI advances. I must say that it was very challenging to launch a product like the Vision Pro, so the odds are in Apple's favor to deliver a better Siri soon with the ability and experience of Mr. Rockwell.

Another reason why pundits consider Apple behind in AI is because the company has a more secretive R&D culture, as it rarely publishes AI research or participates in academic competitions, which makes its AI advances less visible to the broader community. Conversely, companies like Alphabet (GOOG), Amazon (AMZN), or Meta (META) are continuously involved in major AI conferences, regularly publishing, releasing tools/models, and engaging in open competitions to showcase and refine their AI capabilities.

Nevertheless, that does not mean that Apple is not advancing in AI; it's just that we can not actually see those advancements, as Apple prioritizes intellectual property protection and competitive advantages. For instance, I did not know that Apple was reportedly working on a new smart home device that combines elements of a Pixar-style animated robot and a HomePod-like speaker with a self-moving display, which might be launched in 2026 or 2027 to the market.

It's true that if Apple is not open to sharing its AI advances with the community, those advances will be slower than those of its peers. In fact, there are other restrictions that I will comment on in the next section that are slowing down the pace of Apple's AI advancements.

The reason I am bullish about Apple's long-term fundamentals is the fact that Apple is giving "hope and promise" to its very loyal consumers that these advancements will be refined eventually, and once they are finished, Apple will offer an Apple Intelligence that actually works while offering private security of the user's data, which most of Apple's peers are totally disregarding.

This "hope and promise" are not just words; we'll see later how this works.

Apple's AI requirements are more challenging than those of their main counterparts for good reasons

One of the main reasons why Apple is facing difficulties in developing Apple Intelligence is because Apple wants to deliver part of its AI features on-device to keep user privacy. Indeed, it's much harder to run large AI models locally on phones than in a data center. Companies like Google, Amazon, or Meta trust in their cloud processing, where they can use massive models without restrictions of memory or power constraints; of course, they are more flexible in terms of the user's data privacy.

Of course, not all AI requirements by the user can be handled by the device, so Apple offers its own silicon server in the cloud, the Private Cloud Computing (PCC), to please AI processes that use more computational power. The PCC uses end-to-end encryption, which means that it only processes data necessary for the specific task and then immediately deletes it. I talked about it in my last article:

In my view, it's a brilliant strategy, as Tim Cook did not have to invest a huge amount of money in data centers while not breaking the promise of one of its main commitments or pillars, privacy and security. In this sense, Apple user data was never used to train Apple intelligence, and private cloud computing (PCC) was not used for that purpose either.

In the last WWDC, Apple talked about how its AI would be mainly on-device, and eventually, when the user needs more complex requirements, the user would have the alternative of using the PCC, which is a cloud designed specifically for private AI processing. This means that the personal user data sent to the PCC is not accessible to anyone, not even Apple. Furthermore, PCC deletes the user's data after fulfilling the request, while no user data is stored in any form after the response is returned.

In addition, Apple's AI capabilities need to work seamlessly across the different Apple products with different chipsets, memory capacities, and power constraints, which makes necessary a model optimization to run at scale across a huge number of active devices worldwide. These AI features need to be extremely polished, privacy-compliant, and seamlessly integrated.

This more conservative approach slows down Apple's pace to launch new AI features, putting it behind its main peers in generative AI, but Apple's AI strategy is fundamentally different from its peers, with Apple's AI strategy being more focused on privacy, hardware and software integration, and on-device performance. That's why I think it's a serious mistake to compare Apple with its main peers in terms of its AI strategy.

Pundits and bear analysts tend to overlook how important privacy is for Apple's users; that's why they wrongly take a bear case with respect to Apple, as they focus more on the short term than on the long term, which is actually the usual horizon in any Apple project, as Tim Cook has repeated so many times in the last decade. The amazing returns of long-term investors of Apple can testify to what I am saying.

Apple's strategy for AI over the long haul: take advantage of the AI's peers

In my last article, I mentioned that Apple was training its data for AI using Google's and Amazon's data centers; indeed, Apple used Google's Tensor Processing Units (TPUs) and both cloud and on-premise GPUs. TPUs are chips designed for artificial intelligence that are rented by Google through its cloud service as an alternative to Nvidia graphics processing units.

Apple also used Amazon's custom AI chips to enhance the efficiency of Apple's machine learning workloads, including search services and pre-training AI models for Apple Intelligence. Most investors think that Apple does not have its own data centers, but it's wrong; Apple owns data centers, having invested billions in the US and internationally, but those centers are entirely focused on powering Apple services such as iCloud, Siri, iMessage, Apple Music, etc.

Those Apple data centers are not prepared for large-scale model training, so it is more convenient for Apple to use data centers from other companies like Google or Amazon, as they offer scalable and on-demand access to high-performance computer clusters. If Apple had similar data centers to Amazon or Google, it would require many years of training data under cutting-edge AI models from scratch.

It's way easier to train the data based on Apple's demand at any moment with high flexibility, avoiding building permanent infrastructure that Apple might not use all the time. Probably, you are asking yourself that if Apple is using a third-party infrastructure to train its data, how Apple is actually protecting the data for the use of that third party.

Apple solved this problem by designing systems that prevent providers from getting access to Apple's data; for instance, encrypting training data or using strict security policies. This is what Tim Cook calls a "hybrid strategy," which means for Apple to use its own infrastructure and third-party infrastructure for the further development of Apple Intelligence.

This hybrid strategy is not only related to the data centers to train data but also related to AI assistance in Apple's devices. In my last article, I mentioned that Apple offers an integration with ChatGPT, which actually makes a difference from going directly to ChatGPT, mostly related to the user's privacy and security.

Some days ago, Google confirmed that it is close to reaching a deal with Apple to integrate Gemini into the iPhones, something similar to the kind of relationship with OpenAI. Recently, there was news that Apple is partnering with Alibaba (BABA) to roll out AI for iPhones sold in China. Few people are paying attention to this Apple strategy, and I think that it's key to developing a bullish case for Apple over the long haul; indeed, it seems like Apple would be in a privileged position over the long haul to choose its AI partners to take advantage of its more than 2.3 billion active devices worldwide.

In this sense, as Apple keeps developing and improving Apple Intelligence, there will be other AI partners that will fill those "holes" not covered by Apple Intelligence. In the long term, we could imagine that most of Apple's peers will make huge investments in AI and data centers to stay ahead, whereas Apple will end up selecting the "best AI partners."

Long-term trends in AI privacy

As AI developments keep advancing, regulators will be more concerned about how companies handle the user's data. Currently, it's common to see companies managing the data centralized in their data centers, so I can infer that regulators will be more comfortable with AI learning in a decentralized way; for instance, on-device AI like Apple's strategy with Apple Intelligence, which combines on-device AI and PCC with high security of the privacy data.

On the other hand, another trend that can strongly emerge in the future is audited AI, which is the practice of assessing, mitigating, and assuring an algorithm's safety, legality, and security. In addition, regulators would pursue that users have better control of their data to opt in or opt out of AI learning from their own data. In any scenario, Apple will be the best prepared out of its main counterparts to avoid future fines or substantial regulatory changes that could threaten its AI processes and services.

Perplexity App

The "hope and promise" I mentioned earlier relies on the fact that Apple still offers very good alternatives until Apple Intelligence is completely refined and polished. One of those alternatives is the third-party app, Perplexity, which can be downloaded from the AppStore.

Perplexity is an AI-powered search and answer assistant, similar to ChatGPT or Gemini, designed for rapid, accurate, and cited answers to any question. I was impressed watching videos on YouTube about how Perplexity can be used; for instance, you can tell it, using your voice, to remind you to send an important message to a colleague at a specific time, or you can ask about your schedule in the next week, and Perplexity will explore all your schedule and give you all the meetings and things to be done on a specific day.

In addition, in case you have to change anything in your schedule next week, you can ask Perplexity to make the changes, moving meetings or events to other days or hours in the same day. Also, you can ask Perplexity to search for a specific song or playlist, and you can ask for any curiosity or story behind a specific song and singer, and Perplexity will answer it almost immediately.

Perplexity can not replace Siri in all aspects, as Perplexity does not control iPhone settings, apps, or system functions. For instance, only Siri will be able to search a specific photo in your social media, though it's not able to do so yet. However, Perplexity is a very interesting complement to answering complex questions or research, supporting Apple's users with complex AI tasks while waiting for a more refined Siri and Apple Intelligence.

Valuation

According to SA, Apple receives a valuation grade of "F," which means that in every valuation multiple used, Apple stock is more expensive than its peers. However, I take these ratios with a grain of salt, as most of them do not take into account the quality and predictability of the business model; indeed, when you compare the valuation ratios with those of the peers, you are assuming that all the group has the same quality of the business model, a similar quality of the management, etc.

If a company has a very predictable business model with solid competitive advantages, I prefer to use the DCF, which is more aligned with my long-term view. In addition, we need a method that incorporates Vision Pro's growth prospects and the share buyback's impact on Apple's valuation, which is a very important factor that is mostly overlooked. As such, I propose a DCF model that will include both important factors to get a better approximation of Apple's intrinsic value.

Assumptions

  • Outstanding shares (diluted): 15,408 million FY 2024 (based on this figure I've made a projection for 2030 assuming a reduction of 3% each year based on the average of the same rate last years; these projected outstanding shares FY 2030 will be used for the final enterprise value to be divided by these outstanding shares projected to incorporate the consistent and predictable Apple's share buybacks.)
  • FCF margins: 26% (average of the last 10 years).
  • Revenue growth according to consensus
  • Cash as of March 2025: $28,162 million.
  • Debt as of March 2025: $78,566 million.
  • Discount rate: 7.5%. (Considering that Apple has a strong business model coupled with the fact that there could be interest rate reductions by the FED at some point in the next 4 years of the Trump administration)
  • FCF growth in perpetuity: 3.5% annual (CAGR FCF growth from 2019 to 2024: 7.8%).

  • I use the Vision Pro's assumptions made by BofA Securities analyst Wamsi Mohan and UBS analyst David Vogt. I used those assumptions as a base case, but I've made adjustments, reducing expectations.

We should focus first on "green" intrinsic value, which does not include the Vision Pro and the buybacks effect. To find the perpetuity, we used the formula

Perpetuity = FCF 2027/(discounted rate - g).

where g = FCF growth in perpetuity, which was assumed to be 9% annually.

The FCFs are calculated by multiplying the revenues projected by the FCF margins assumed at 26%; in this way, I get the FCFs estimated for the next few years.

With perpetuity, we calculate the present value of all the FCFs beyond 2026. Then, we calculate the enterprise value using the following:

Enterprise Value = Present Value of FCF (from 2025 to 2026) + Perpetuity + Cash - Total Debt.

Finally, the intrinsic value is calculated by taking the enterprise value and dividing it by the outstanding number of shares. In this way, we could get $187 per share as an intrinsic value under the assumptions presented.

Now, in June 2024, when I wrote my last article, I used the estimations of BofA Securities analyst Wamsi Mohan and UBS analyst David Vogt for the projections of the Vision Pro units sold, which was estimated to be 400 units in 2024 for the first year. It seems that those estimations were very close to the actual units sold in 2024, according to some analysts. So, I kept the 400 units sold in 2024 with respect to the assumptions I made in my last article in June 2024.

However, for the years beyond 2024, I reduced annual sales growth of the Vision Pro from 40%CAGR in my article in June 2024 to 20% CAGR this time, considering more cheaper versions, as it was reported in October 2024 that Apple was working on more cheaper versions, so the goal here is to find a present value of the FCF generated by the Vision Pro sales:

I assume an FCF margin of 20% for the Vision Pro, which is lower than the 26% FCF margins of Apple's overall sales. Being aware of how demanding Tim Cook is with Apple's manufacturing efficiency, I think that is a reasonable FCF margin. Then, I calculate the present value of all the FCFs to be generated in the future by the Vision Pro to be added to the enterprise value previously calculated.

Thanks to the Vision Pro FCF generation over the years, we see that our intrinsic value has increased from $187 to $193 per share. Finally, I make a projection of the outstanding number of shares until 2030, considering a 3% rate of reduction in the share count. So, if I take the sum of the enterprise value of Apple's FCF without Vision Pro plus the present value of the FCF generated by Vision Pro's sales and divide them by the share count projected for 2030, I'll get an intrinsic value of $220 per share. If we consider the outstanding shares projected for the year 2035, assuming that we will hold the shares for the next 10 years, the intrinsic value can reach $251 per share.

This intrinsic value might be higher if Apple accelerates its share buyback policy at some point in the future, if the Vision Pro sales achieve good penetration into its niche segment, or if Apple manages to fully integrate Apple Intelligence in iPhones, offering privacy and security that consumers are looking for and that no other competitor offers. That's why I consider the stock a buy to let several positive catalysts trigger the share price in the next years.

Apple: High-quality business

In Apple's case, an investor should take into account that the margin of safety not only is the difference between the current price and intrinsic value but also is the high-quality business model and its long-term predictability as you can see in the next table:

Most bear analysts focus on only the growth as if Apple were a "growth stock"; that's a mistake since a company selling mostly physical products in a mature market can not be qualified as growth. However, in the last decade, we see high and growing returns on capital, growing gross margins, growing EPS most of the time, growing dividends per share, high and consistent FCF margins, and low debt levels, indicating that Apple is not like any other mature company; it's special and unique while constantly adding value over the years.

Risks

Some risks to my bull thesis would be the impact of tariffs on Apple's sales, but I am not concerned about them. Indeed, Tim Cook has estimated a tariff-related cost of $900 million per quarter under certain assumptions, according to what he said in the last call for the results of Q2 FY2025. If we take that number to turn into an annual figure, we get $3.6 billion of annual cost; now, considering $100 billion of FCF per year, the tariff-related costs would only represent around 3% of the FCF per year. That's an affordable cost for Apple and its very loyal users.

On the other hand, I like to see that Tim Cook is taking action to reduce the chance that tariffs can go up again, using a smart strategy to avoid it:

The existing tariffs that apply to Apple today are based on the product's country of origin as you alluded to. For the June quarter, we do expect the majority of iPhones sold in the U.S. will have India as their country of origin and Vietnam to be the country of origin for almost all iPad, Mac, Apple Watch, and AirPods products sold in the -- also sold in the U.S. China would continue to be the country of origin for the vast majority of total product sales outside the U.S.

That means that most of Apple's devices would come from India and Vietnam to avoid any possibility of more tariffs on imports to the US from China. China still will be the main source of manufacturing for all the countries outside the US. That's the kind of flexibility in logistics that comes from Cook's ability to be truly prepared for any material change in the global scenarios.

A risk frequently mentioned by bear analysts is about the deal between Google and Apple that implies a $20 billion fee paid to Apple to enable Google search to be the default search engine on Safari. As such, if the DOJ determines that this deal is illegal, Apple will be impacted materially as it will not receive the annual $20 billion payment from Google, substantially reducing the growth prospects of its services segment.

Nevertheless, I am not concerned about it either since even in the worst-case scenario where Google will no longer pay Apple the $20 billion payment for being the default search engine, Apple still can sign another new multi-billion-dollar deal with Google focused on integrating Gemini AI as a strategic pivot, as I've mentioned earlier, including contingent clauses to secure at least a $20 billion payment. As a result, no matter what the final result of the scrutiny of Google's payments is, there are very high chances that Apple still receives its $20 billion annually in any scenario.

Other risks could be related to the fact that Mark Rockwell leaving the Vision Products Group within Apple to be the new leader of Siri could slow down the further development of the Vision Pro. According to some information, Rockwell is being replaced by Paul Meade, who has a lot of experience in spatial computing hardware and who managed hardware engineering for Vision Pro under Rockwell's leadership. So, I would give him some credit that he will continue with the outstanding results of Rockwell in Vision Pro.

Finally, another risk might be related to the decline in sales in China, which is an important market for Apple as it represents 16% of the total sales as of March 2025. I think that the media tends to exaggerate while being more focused on the short-term expectations. I think that Apple is taking very interesting steps to reinforce its position in China, like partnering with Alibaba and JD.com to integrate their apps into the Vision Pro, enhancing the headset's appeal, and addressing the competitive challenges from local rivals.

In addition, Apple and Alibaba have worked jointly to develop AI features for iPhones in China, submitting them for regulatory approval. Thus, I think that we will see in the next years how Apple gets more partnerships to leverage the partners' resources to recover its market position. Of course, the rollout of Apple Intelligence will help enormously to call the attention of many users that would like to be part of Apple's ecosystem once Apple Intelligence is at full capacity.

Takeaways

I think that it was necessary to mention in this article the complexities faced by Apple related to its AI strategy, but once those obstacles are overcome, Apple will have even stronger competitive advantages related not only to the brand but also to the way the company protects the user's data while being effective in its AI processes.

We need to remember that, at Apple, privacy and security of the user's data come first before any innovation, so an Apple Intelligence fully integrated into Apple's devices that actually protects users' data will be strongly prepared, more than any other big tech, for the future challenges associated with regulatory purposes related to protecting the user's data in any AI process.

data.title: Match Group: Tinder Can't Stop Losing Paid Users, Creating A Vicious Cycle
data.description:
Even though the stock market has rebounded sharply from recent lows, we shouldn't let our guard down. The harsh reality is that we're either sitting in a recession or turning around the corner into one, and amid that backdrop, there's a good chance that companies that were already struggling to begin with will steer into even choppier waters.

Match Group, Inc. (NASDAQ:MTCH), the parent company behind Tinder and Hinge, sits squarely in this conundrum. The world's largest dating app conglomerate continues to shed paying users in its biggest segment. The company's Q1 results showcased a very disappointing decline in revenue, and expectations of a continued decline throughout the year, leading to a ~10% tumble in the stock.

I last wrote a bearish note on Match in February, when the stock was trading closer to $33 per share. Since then, Match has continued to see downside price action, which has helped its valuation multiples. At the same time, new CEO Spencer Rascoff (a respected internet sector executive who is the former CEO of Zillow, and the founder of Hotwire) is making decisive moves to reshape the organization, including a huge 13% reduction in workforce. Though I see this belt-tightening as incrementally positive, it's tough to overlook the massive exodus in Match's paid user base. As such, I'm reiterating my sell opinion on Match.

Part of the core issue here is that, in my view, Match is positioned to continue disappointing. As a reminder, the company recently hosted an investor day in December 2024 that laid out a three-year return-to-growth framework, calling for a 4-6% year/y revenue growth CAGR through FY25 and a 39% adjusted operating income target.

As Match moves into a recessionary economy at a time when consumers were already pushing back against paying for these products, the company's long-term targets look even further out of reach. As a reminder, the core components of Match's 4-6% growth CAGR rest on a return to low single-digit growth for Tinder, and a ~20% growth CAGR for Hinge. The company failed against both targets in Q1, despite lower operating margins for Tinder which suggests the company is throwing plenty of marketing behind its key brand.

We don't think the dating-app space is subject to pent-up demand, either. In a recession, consumers may put off purchases of big-ticket items like homes, furniture, and cars, or delay expenses like big vacations, but these purchases may bounce back after the recessionary period is over. We can hardly say the same about paid subscriptions: as Match loses momentum, we can't count on payers to come back.

Steer clear here and invest elsewhere.

Q1 download

Let's now go through Match's latest quarterly results in greater detail. The Q1 earnings summary is shown below:

Match’s overall company revenue declined -3% y/y to $831 million, slightly beating Wall Street’s expectations of $827 million (-4% y/y). Direct revenue dragged -4% y/y to $812 million, though we note Match faced two points of FX headwinds from the stronger dollar in Q1. The bright side on this front: the sharp weakening of the dollar in Q2 following President Trump’s tariff announcements should help Match’s optical growth rates improve.

That’s where the hope cuts short, however. Match’s largest segment, Tinder, continues to see a big exodus of paid users. Tinder, which is still over half of Match’s overall revenue, saw its direct revenue decline -7% y/y to $447.4 million, as shown in the chart below. Perhaps even more troublesome is the fact that the company saw yet another sharp reduction in payers, shedding 0.4 million paid accounts to end at 9.1 million payers.

As a reminder, Match’s core strategy to reverse declines at Tinder was to step up requirements for account creation, hoping that this would increase the number of quality daters on the platform and reduce Tinder’s stigma as a hookup app. The company also hoped that new AI features such as an automated photo selector would help users to overcome the new hurdles associated with account creation. The company’s sudden spike in users in Q3 seemed to validate that strategy, but the past two quarters proved that the Q3 increase was more of a head fake. We note as well that the Tinder segment’s adjusted operating margins fell sequentially to 49%, weaker than a range of 51-53% in the past three quarters, indicating that despite Match spending more on its flagship product, the brand is flailing anyway.

It has usually been the case that a slowdown in Tinder was compensated by huge growth in Hinge, but while Hinge continues to grow, its growth pace has moderated considerably. As shown in the chart below, direct revenue at Hinge grew 23% y/y to $152.2 million, but this also decelerated quite sharply from 27% growth in Q4. Also, Hinge only picked up 0.1 million paid users in Q4, or about a lot of quarter of what Tinder lost. Granted, Hinge users are more valuable than Tinder payers (with an RPP of $29.90 almost double that of Tinder’s $16.38, mainly due to the fact that Tinder has gone international while Hinge is still largely a domestic brand with high subscription and add-on prices) - but the point stands that Hinge’s growth is no longer sufficient to pick up slack for the overall company.

We note that Match’s performance in its core brands falls far short of the longer-term targets the company has set. Hinge needs a 20% growth CAGR through FY27 to fit into the company’s overall 4-6% growth framework, which means there’s little room for deceleration after this quarter. Tinder is far from a return to single-digit growth.

Meanwhile, management notes that macro clouds may be darkening growth trends even further for Tinder. While the company notes that subscription trends remain sticky, the company has begun to notice a drop-off in à la carte purchases. Per CFO Steven Bailey’s remarks on the Q1 earnings call:

Our business is not directly subject to tariffs. And because a significant portion of our revenue is derived from subscriptions, which tend to be stickier than impulse purchases like a la carte, our business has historically been relatively resilient to macroeconomic impacts. We've seen some impacts to ALC revenue in the past, especially at our brands with younger users or those with less discretionary income, and we've started to see some impacts to ALC revenue at Tinder in recent weeks, which we are monitoring closely. We are prepared to take pricing, merchandising or other actions to minimize the impact on our financial performance should these trends persist.

The recent decline in the dollar relative to other major currencies helped our Q1 results, and we expect FX to be a tailwind to year-over-year total revenue growth in Q2, helping to offset any consumer spending-related headwinds.”

Valuation and key takeaways

The one benefit to Match is that the stock remains cheap. Match trades at a ~8x forward-adjusted EBITDA multiple, richer than Bumble (which is struggling even more with growth) but meaningfully below Grindr Inc. (GRND), which seems to be bucking the payer declines at its two larger rivals.

But in my view, consistent bleeding in Match’s paid user counts dooms the stock to be a value trap. Whatever momentum Match may have temporarily enjoyed following its aggressive December Investor Day goals and the recharged leadership of a prominent CEO in Spencer Rascoff, has dramatically subsided. While I applaud many of the new leadership team’s moves (the belt-tightening, plus Rascoff’s announcement of his own insider share purchases), I believe Match is beyond saving. Recent declines in membership are yet another signal that we’ve reached peak dating app fatigue, while macroeconomic conditions are going to further sap younger users’ willingness and ability to pay for premium features.

Steer clear here and invest elsewhere.

data.title: First Look: Fidelity Disruptive Automation ETF
data.description:
Introduction

Five years ago, Fidelity launched five “disruptive” ETFs to hold stocks they deemed to be changing the industry that the ETF focused on. The third of that set I chose to review is the Fidelity Disruptive Automation ETF (NASDAQ:FBOT). I plan to cover others over the next couple of months. While better than previous reviews of other disruptive ETFs in the series, I still decided to give this ETF a Sell rating.

Fidelity Disruptive Automation ETF Review

Seeking Alpha describes this ETF as:

The fund invests in stocks of companies operating across information technology, software and services, software, systems software, software research and artificial intelligence software sectors. It invests in growth and value stocks of companies across diversified market capitalization. It employs fundamental and quantitative analysis with a bottom-up stock picking approach to create its portfolio. The fund seeks to benchmark the performance of its portfolio against the MSCI ACWI (All Country World Index) Index and the MSCI All Country World Industrials Equal Weighted Index. Fidelity Disruptive Automation ETF was formed on April 16, 2020.

FBOT has $100MM in AUM and comes with 50 basis points in fees. As with the other ETFs in this series, the payout is very irregular which will be shown later.

The ETF’s Prospectus offers this insight into the selection criteria:

Fidelity's disruptive strategies seek to identify innovative developments that could signal new directions for delivering products and services to customers. Companies within the disruptive automation theme include but are not limited to those companies that, in the Adviser's opinion, are engaged in designing and manufacturing automation, enabling technology, tools, or processes including robotics, artificial intelligence, machine vision, process sensors, pneumatic systems, autonomous driving & electric vehicles, and 3D printing. In pursuing this investment theme, the fund may invest in companies in any economic sector.

Source: FBOT Prospectus.

On June 9, 2023, FBOT converted from a mutual fund to an ETF but kept the same investment approach.

Holdings Review

Unlike the other two in this series, FBOT is very international in its holdings, accounting for over 55% of the portfolio.

Asia places second on a regional basis (38%) to North America (46%), with Europe accounting for most of the rest (14%). When looking at market-cap, over 60% are considered giant or large in size, with medium absorbing most of the rest of the allocations.

This makes this ETF more dependent on its larger companies than the other disruptive ETFs covered so far. Next, we see that the top three industries account for 60% of the portfolio.

While high, that is less concentrated than the other ETFs reviewed so far. These 15 stocks (ignoring the Cash lending, Net Assets), out of 51, represent 51% of the portfolio. Compared to the other disruptive ETFs, that is a lower concentration level.

Based on a review of the entire portfolio, either the Fidelity SLCC Fund or Net Assets should be listed as a short position, not long, and the site says there are three assets held that are short positions.

Distributions Review

Fidelity lists each payment being 100% from ordinary income, including the outsized ones.

Performance and Comparison Review

To see if using their disruptive criteria is a useful strategy, I compared it to the Global X Robotics & Artificial Intelligence ETF (BOTZ) and the iShares MSCI ACWI ETF (ACWI), which was listed as one of the benchmarks FBOT uses.

Est CAGR 1.8% -2.6% 13.2%
Year FBOT BOTZ ACWI
2021 20.7% 8.7% 18.7%
2022 -32.5% -42.7% -8.9%
2023 23.1% 39.0% 22.3%
2024 12.6% 12.3% 17.5%
2025 -4.4% -8.7% 1.1%

Of those reviewed so far, this is the first Fidelity Disruptive ETF to perform better than the selected sector benchmark, but that might reflect industry differences as I could not find a distinctive match. Since FBOT failed to even come close to what ACWI provided to investors, I still feel FBOT deserves a Sell rating, leaning toward the Strong Sell the others got.

For those who like the concept anyway, here are a few points to consider:

  • Look at the largest holdings. Would you classify what they do as disruptive? Unlike some other disruptive ETFs, here the top holdings are more diverse.
  • When reviewing how the Fidelity team defines disruptive companies, are you comfortable with it?
  • Any sector-focused ETF comes with the risk that even if their selection criteria work, it could be in an underperforming sector.
  • Holders need to consider if there is a concentration risk.

Portfolio Strategy

The first decision an investor needs to ponder is whether the holdings of the disruptive ETF match their idea of how that concept works. That said, if the performance is beating a standard ETF for that industry, that might be enough to warrant a Buy rating for them.

I look at active or focused ETFs, like FBOT, as owning for the Alpha they might add to my portfolio versus owning an ETF like ACWI that I would consider as a long-term hold fund, which I label as a Core holding. Here, I find that FBOT fails to add Alpha, so I would look elsewhere if I wanted to add to that sector exposure in my overall equity allocations.

data.title: Nvidia Stock Is Down 21% From Its Peak. Is It Time To Buy?
data.description:
NVDA Enters Uncertain Territory Ahead of Earnings Season

Artificial intelligence is helping resuscitate San Francisco after the city’s tech economy was hit hard by the pandemic, according to Nvidia (NASDAQ:NVDA) CEO Jensen Huang. “It’s because of AI that San Francisco is back,” he said while speaking to the U.S. Under Secretary of State for Economic Growth, Energy, and the Environment Jacob Helberg at a forum last week. But as Huang declares an AI renaissance in The Golden City, is Nvidia thriving just as much?

The ailing chipmaker is down nearly 21% from its recent peak of $149.42 on January 6 as growing competition from China's DeepSeek (DEEPSEEK) and a contentious trade war between the U.S. and China pushed its stock lower. The tariff headwinds could knock its China revenue by $15-20B, Bank of America Securities’ Vivek Arya predicted in a research note on Tuesday, casting a shadow over its upcoming May 28 earnings report.

That comes after NVDA informed major Chinese clients, including ByteDance (BDNCE), Alibaba Group (BABA), and Tencent (OTCPK:TCEHY), that it is modifying its AI chips to comply with U.S. export restrictions, which it estimates will result in a $5.5B hit to its Q1 results. As U.S. lawmakers continue to crack down on shipments to China, Huang has sounded the alarm on Chinese rival Huawei Technologies’ competing AI processing technology, writing in a blog post on X:

Regardless of how one feels about DeepSeek's open-source R1 model, it is a clear indication that innovation is moving rapidly around the world, with or without leading U.S. tech. If U.S. platforms are absent, companies will turn to strategic competitors like Huawei to fill the gap. This is why leadership in Al depends not just on what we restrict—but on what we enable.

NVDA is not alone in its struggles. All seven members of the Magnificent Seven stocks are underperforming the S&P 500 this year in a stark reversal from 2024, Creative Planning chief marketing strategist Charlie Bilello noted on his blog last week.

Mag 7 Companies Lag the S&P

It’s not all doom and gloom for NVDA and its Mag 7 peers. The Roundhill Magnificent Seven ETF (MAGS), which offers equal exposure across each of the seven companies, has seen forward revenues and forward earnings climb to record highs this year, underscoring the stocks’ enduring dominance over the market. The Mag Seven represents 28.4% of the S&P 500’s market capitalization, 22.6% of the S&P’s forward revenues, and 11.8% of forward earnings.

Magnificent Seven hit new highs in earnings power despite market struggles in 2025

The question is whether that dominance will be enough to see NVDA through this difficult period. Uncertainty continued to loom over the market after the Federal Reserve opted to keep rates unchanged at 4.25%–4.50% during its May policy meeting this week, emphasizing caution due to trade policy uncertainties and negative Q1 GDP growth.

Seeking Alpha’s Quant System awards the semiconductor giant an ‘A-’ in terms of growth, with forward revenue growth of 59.6% remaining strong at a 781.9% difference to the wider technology sector. Even so, the company may not return to its former glory at the height of the AI boom. As SA analyst MacroGirl writes:

The Mag 7 stocks, once market leaders, are now laggards with diverse sector performances…[NVDA] grew its market cap by over 500% as demand for semiconductors surged. NVDA will continue to be a great company, but that kind of price growth is very unlikely to repeat now that it is valued at $2.5T. Furthermore, the ban on H20 exports to China has led to approximately $5.5 billion of charges associated with H20 products for inventory, purchase commitments, and related reserves.

Should You Buy Nvidia?

SA’s Quant process relies on identifying and ranking stocks collectively based on value, growth, profitability, EPS Revisions, and momentum metrics vs. the sector, analyzing thousands of data points in real time to help remove emotion from the investment process and offer a more balanced perspective. In NVDA’s case, our algorithm anticipates solid—albeit less explosive—growth from NVDA once markets stabilize. Now comes the big question: Is that growth worth $118.38 per share?

NVDA Quant Summary (as of 5/8/25)

A major contributor to SA Quant’s HOLD rating on NVDA is its ‘F’ valuation grade. That doesn’t mean it’s all bad. Its forward price-to-earnings (P/E) gap of 26.84 is only a 2.4% premium to the wider technology sector. Meanwhile, its price-to-earnings growth (PEG) ratio of 0.73 comes at a 52% discount to the wider technology sector median, reflecting a decent price for NVDA’s strong growth outlook. However, lower-rated metrics, including a price-to-sales ratio of 13.82, a 425.46% difference vs. the 2.63 sector median, suggest that other elements of the stock are overvalued.

And like many of its Mag 7 peers, NVDA’s P/E ratio has compressed significantly this year.

Magnificent 7 Stocks’ P/E Multiples Dwindle in 2025

Even so, Nvidia and its fellow Mag 7 stocks are still far from cheap. Meanwhile, the gap between the Mag 7 companies' EPS and the broader S&P 500 is swiftly narrowing, “and is expected to be as little as 2% by Q4 as Mag 7 earnings growth slows,” as MacroGirl points out in their analysis.

Mag 7 Stocks’ EPS Converges With The Broader Market

As Mag 7 returns increasingly lag the S&P and the earnings gap between the two dwindles, it becomes difficult to justify buying an expensive stock like NVDA, even with its highly rated PEG ratio among other positive factors.

SA’s Quant System Can Help Find The Better Buy

Just as I used SA’s Quant system to assess whether to buy NVDA, you can use it to search for better options. Our Top Technology Stocks Screener offers plenty of Strong Buy-rated options with cheaper valuations and higher growth projections.

Using SA Quant’s Top Technology Stocks screener, three Strong Buy alternatives to NVDA (currently ranked #104) include CommScope Holding Company, Inc. (COMM) (#2), Credo Technology Group (CRDO) (#5), and Intapp Inc. (INTA) (#22).

CommScope Holding Company, Inc. (COMM)

  • ‘A+’ valuation grade with a forward GAAP P/E of 4.02, an 84.7% discount from the sector median

  • Forward PEG of 0.14, a 91.2% discount from the sector median

  • 5 upward EPS revisions and 5 upward revenue revisions

Credo Technology Group (CRDO)

  • Forward PEG (price-to-earnings growth) of 0.62, a 58% discount from the sector median

  • ‘A+’ growth grade underlined by forward revenue growth of 55.3%, a 712% difference from the sector median.

  • 10 positive EPS revisions and zero negative EPS revisions over the past 90 days

Intapp Inc. (INTA)

  • ‘A+’ forward EPS diluted growth of 10%, a 992% discount from the sector median

  • ‘A+’ growth grade and ‘A’ momentum grade

  • 10 positive EPS revisions and zero negative EPS revisions over the past 90 days

There are plenty more Strong Buy tech companies to explore. You can also narrow your search further, filtering for industries such as Systems Software, Interactive Media & Services, Semiconductors, and more to find highly rated investment opportunities with less risk than certain overvalued tech stocks.

Why Buy NVDA When There Are Less Risky Alternatives?

As NVDA approaches its latest earnings release, uncertainty from tariffs, geopolitical tensions, and Chinese AI adversaries like DeepSeek and Huawei hangs in the air. Despite some positive valuation characteristics, such as its highly discounted PEG, SA’s Quant System rates NVDA’s valuation as an ‘F’ as the current market environment signals a difficult road ahead for the Mag 7. Seeking Alpha’s Quant System can help you assess hundreds of financial metrics of Strong Buy-rated stocks to identify less risky options that best suit your portfolio.

If you're seeking a limited number of monthly ideas from the hundreds of top quant Strong Buy-rated stocks, consider exploring Alpha Picks. Alpha Picks employs a transparent and systematic strategy that combines the expertise of Seeking Alpha’s Quantitative Team, our unique data, and the computational power of the systems we have built.

data.title: TLT Vs. VCTL: Yield Spread Is Near 10-Year Low
data.description:
TLT vs. VCLT ETF: previous thesis and new development

I last analyzed the iShares 20+ Year Treasury Bond ETF (NASDAQ:TLT) about 2 months ago in mid-March 2025. That article was titled “TLT Vs. EDV: Why I Prefer EDV And How I Allocate It Dynamically.” The article explained how we dynamically adjust the bond-equity ratio and rated TLT as a hold for the following considerations:

I use a dynamic allocation method based on bonds’ valuations relative to other assets. The current spread between SP500 CAPE yield and long-term treasury rates is toward the thinner end of the historical spectrum. This indicates attractive valuation of long-term bonds like those held in TLT and EDV relative to equity. Among these two ETFs, I prefer EDV due to its lower fees, which can more than offset its higher trading friction given my frequent portfolio adjustments.

As for the Vanguard Long-Term Corporate Bond Index Fund ETF Shares (NASDAQ:VCLT), I last analyzed this ETF about 1 month ago in late March 2025. That article was titled “VGLT Vs. VCLT: This Chart Shows Now Is The Wrong Time For Corporate Bonds.” It rated VCLT as a sell after the following consideration of the credit spread between corporate bonds and risk-free rates:

VCLT presents an unattractive reward/risk profile compared to VGLT due to several considerations. The top one is that the credit spread between corporate bonds and treasury rates is near its thinnest level since the 1980s. In addition, VGLT could offer potential tax benefits for many investors.

Since the publication of those articles, the macroeconomic landscape has shifted materially and motivated a reexamination of both ETFs. More specifically, the tariff disputes that started on April 2, 2025 (aka the "Liberation Day") and also the inflation data released then have caused the market to reassess interest rate scenarios. As a result of these changes, the Federal Reserve’s latest decision (announced on Wednesday, May 7) was to keep Fed rates unchanged while issuing a caution that both unemployment and inflation could increase if tariff disputes escalate.

Under this backdrop, the thesis of this article is twofold. First, I will explain why the bond market is severely underestimating the risks behind corporate bonds such as those held in VCLT, given the ongoing macroeconomic uncertainties. Second, I will explain that treasury bonds such as TLT offer one of the most attractive return/risk ratios in a decade relative to VCLT. With these considerations, this article maintains my hold rating on TLT and sell rating on VCLT.

TLT and VCLT ETF: basic facts

To facilitate the subsequent analyses, let me take a minute to go over the basics of both VCLT and TLT. As already stated in the names, both funds target the long end of the bond maturity spectrum. TLT, issued by BlackRock, focuses on treasury bonds with 20 years of remaining maturity or more, and VCLT is indexed on corporate bonds with maturities greater than 10 years as detailed in their fund description below.

TLT fund description: TLT tracks a market-weighted index of debt issued by the US Treasury with remaining maturities of 20 years or more. TLT effectively captures the far end of the Treasury curve in a liquid package. Exclusively holding bonds with 20+ years to maturity, TLT is by design very sensitive to long-term interest-rate movements. TLT is a great choice for investors who want long-term Treasury exposure.

VCLT fund description: VCLT holds a broad portfolio of long-term investment-grade corporate bonds. Like all long-term bond funds, it is highly sensitive to long-term interest rates, but its high weighted-average credit rating means that its credit risk is very small. The fund includes USD-denominated, fixed-rate, taxable securities issued by industrial, utility, and financial companies with more than 10 years until maturity.

Both funds are taxable ETFs, although note that dividend income from TLT could be exempted from state tax as federal obligations. TLT has a higher expense ratio of 0.15% with a much larger AUM of $47.01 billion. In contrast, VCLT features Vanguard’s signature lower expense ratio of 0.03%. It has a much smaller, but still sizable, AUM of $13.16 billion.

TLT-VCLT yield spread is near a 10-year low

To illustrate the risk premium of TLT relative to VCLT, the following chart displays the dividend yield spread between the VCLT and TLT in the past 10 years. Over the past decade, the spread has fluctuated widely, reaching a high of 2.929 in the wake of the pandemic and a low of 0.838 in 2024. But overall, the spread has been tractable and range-bound around an average of 1.664 most of the time as seen. The current spread is only 1.087, not only far below the average but also among the lowest levels in at least 10 years. I believe such a thin spread illustrates an unusually unfavorable return/risk ratio for VCLT relative to treasury bonds.

As to be further elaborated next, VCLT valuation risks are further heightened by its credit exposure and also the macroeconomic uncertainties.

Other risks and final thoughts

The chart below shows a breakdown of the credit exposure of VCLT as of March 31, 2025. To echo a point mentioned earlier, the exposure is predominantly in investment-grade corporate bonds. The largest allocation is to BBB-rated bonds, making up 45.00% of the fund. Bonds rated A constituted the next largest portion at 43.50%. Higher-rated bonds comprise a minor portion of the funds with AA-rated bonds at 9.30%, AAA bonds at 2.10%, and a minimal exposure of 0.20% to U.S. Government securities.

The significant exposure to medium-quality corporate debt within the VCLT portfolio, when combined with the fund’s long duration, could cause large downside corrections in the case of a market downturn. If the overall economy weakens (for example, as a result of escalated tariff disputes), I expect the companies with weaker credit ratings to be impacted more severely due to their limited financial resources.

On the positive side, VCLT’s diversification offers some degree of protection against such downside. As seen in the next chart, TLT only holds 43 securities, with a significant 47.94% of its assets concentrated in its top 10 holdings. Investors have no need to be alarmed by such concentrated exposure. TLT focuses on treasury bonds only, and the credit risk of one treasury bond is the same as the credit risk of all treasury bonds. In contrast, VCLT is much more diversified, holding 2790 securities to diversify the credit risks. As seen, it has only 2.60% of its assets invested in its top 10 holdings.

data.title: Champion Homes: Sitting On The Sidelines
data.description:
Nobody has a perfect track record when it comes to investing. But I feel like I have a pretty solid one. One company that I could point to as an example of this is Champion Homes (NYSE:SKY), an enterprise that produces manufactured homes from the manufacturing facilities that it operates. This is an interesting niche of the housing market that's often overlooked by investors. The reason why I say I have a good track record with this business is because of how my rating calls on it have played out over time. You see, whenever I rate a company a "hold," I'm making the claim that I believe that shares are unlikely to perform materially different than the broader market for the foreseeable future. And that's precisely what has been the case here.

Since my last article about the business in October of last year, an article in which I reaffirmed the company as a "hold" candidate, shares have pulled back by 2.6%. This is not materially different than the 3.2% drop the S&P 500 saw. And since I originally rated it a "hold" in October 2022, the stock has soared by 55.6%. But that's barely any different than the 56.4% that the broader market experienced. Looking at the picture today, I see little reason to change my assessment. Shares are still quite pricey compared to many other homebuilders. The firm does have a great balance sheet, but that's not enough to justify matters. Add on top of this concerns about the economy because of the current trade war situation, and I believe that maintaining my current "hold" rating is appropriate.

This does not mean that I can't or won't change my opinion at some point. As new data comes in, I would be crazy not to if it’s warranted. It just so happens that, on May 20, after the market closes, the management team at this homebuilder will be reporting financial results for the final quarter of the company's 2025 fiscal year. Leading up to that point, analysts anticipate revenue and profits all rising nicely year over year. But unless we see something really special happen, I don't see an upgrade as likely.

Still Interesting, Still Pricey

The manufactured housing industry is interesting to me in part because it's a battle between two concepts. The first of these is that because the homes are substantially cheaper than traditional homes, they're properties that technically should appeal to lower income Americans. To see the extent to which this is true, we need only consider that the median sales price for a new house in the U.S. in March of this year was $403,600. That was down from the $436,400 experienced one year earlier, with high interest rates and inflationary pressures hammering demand. To put this in perspective, in the third quarter of its 2025 fiscal year, Champion Homes reported that the average price of a home that it sold in the U.S. was only $94,900. That's a substantial difference that should appeal to those on the lower end of the income and wealth spectrum.

The other opposing concept is that lower income Americans are the least likely to be able to afford purchasing a home anyways. According to Urban Wire, the home ownership rate for the lowest income quintile in the U.S. was 45.2% in 2023. That compares to 65.9% for those in the middle quintile and 85.3% for those in the top. The company thrives by finding that sizable minority of the lowest income Americans and catering to them.

According to management, because of how expensive homes are, 60% of the U.S. population cannot afford a traditional home. This is problematic for those who, like me, advocate for generational wealth-building opportunities. It's also worth pointing out that this is particularly problematic when it comes to younger Americans. Using data from 2021, management showed that 46% of all manufactured homes are owned by Millennials. However, because of lower income in retirement, Baby Boomers represent the second-largest cohort of 26%.

Long term, management seems optimistic about this industry. And this is because there has been considerable under-building in this space since the 1990s. There was a big drop from 1998 until 2009 because of the housing bubble that formed. And ever since that popped, manufactured housing shipments have been on the rise. They still, however, lag significantly behind what they were from the early 1960s through the late 1990s.

The growth experienced in this space has made it possible for Champion Homes to do quite well. Take its most recent quarter, which would be the third quarter of the 2025 fiscal year, as an example. During that time, revenue for the business was $644.9 million. That's an increase of 15.3% compared to the $559.5 million reported one year earlier. Some of this increase was because of a rise in the average price per home that the company sold in the U.S.

That number grew from $92,300 to $94,900. Furthermore, the number of homes delivered in the U.S. expanded from 5,643 to 6,437 during this time. Unfortunately, the number of homes sold in Canada dropped from 249 to 209, while the average price there pulled back from $123,700 to $122,900. But as you can tell, Canada is a much less significant place for Champion Homes and its revenue stream.

The rise in revenue for the company has brought with it higher profits as well. Net income jumped from $47 million in the third quarter of 2024 to $61.5 million in the third quarter of this year. Operating cash flow did drop from $89.5 million to $50.5 million. But if we adjust for changes in working capital, we would get an increase from $59.8 million to $76 million. Finally, EBITDA for the business expanded from $66.3 million to $83.3 million.

In the chart above, you can see financial performance for the first nine months of 2025 compared to the same time in 2024. Revenue, profits and cash flows all improved year over year. The one exception was operating cash flow. But the adjusted figure for it and EBITDA both increased. This came as the number of homes delivered in the U.S. increased and as average pricing grew as well, both of which can be seen in the chart below. All things considered, the picture is looking quite positive.

In this environment, there's definitely uncertainty because of the tariff situation. According to the National Association of Home Builders, the tariffs currently imposed should add around $10,900 to a new homes’ typical construction costs. That number should certainly be lower for the manufactured housing industry because of the smaller size of those homes, and the lower quality materials that they are built with. In its third quarter earnings call, the company did not provide much in the way of detail. All they said was that their margin expectations for the final quarter of the year excluded the potential impact of tariffs. They also mentioned that they are remaining agile during this time and will keep investors updated as the picture changes.

I would venture to say that the firm's margins will probably be impacted some. But I suspect that they will pass on most of the costs onto the buyers of the properties. The way I view this situation is that while there could be a reduction in housing demand in general, including in the manufactured housing industry, what a prolonged period of economic weakness might do is merely shift up the average income of buyers of these properties. This means that those making more money, preferring to buy a cheaper home than a traditional home, will ultimately opt for a manufactured house instead of a traditional one. Those at the lowest end of the income stream, however, will certainly fall off and have to find alternative arrangements.

At present, analysts seem optimistic. After the market closes on May 20, management is going to be announcing financial results for the final quarter of the company's 2025 fiscal year. Analysts currently believe that revenue will be around $595.4 million. That would be up from the $536.4 million reported last year and would likely come from additional unit shipments. They also anticipate earnings per share of $0.77, which would translate to a pretty nice rise over the $0.05 per share reported for the same time last year. This would mean a jump in net income from $2.8 million to $44.7 million.

On an adjusted basis, analysts believe that earnings per share will also be $0.77. That's more than the $0.62 per share in adjusted net income reported last year and would translate to a rise in adjusted net profits from $36 million to $44.7 million. In the table above, you can see what the other important cash flow metrics for the company were in the final quarter of last year. It is highly probable that these will also improve. However, I actually think that there's a chance that operating cash flow might actually drop. I say this because I think that management might have worked hard to stock up on building materials leading up to the implementation of tariffs. I don't have any evidence to support this. But it would be a logical decision.

In terms of valuation, the company is still quite a bit higher than I would like it to be. If we annualize the results that we have seen so far for the year, we would expect net profits of $165.3 million, adjusted operating cash flow of $245.5 million, and EBITDA of $297.2 million. Using these estimates and the historical performance for 2024, you can see in the chart above how shares are currently priced. This pricing is indicative of a company that, in my book, is pretty solidly fairly valued. But compared to other homebuilders, most of which are admittedly traditional ones instead of manufactured ones, shares look pricey. In the table below, you can see what I mean. On a price to earnings basis, Champion Homes ended up being the most expensive of the six companies shown in that table. On a price to operating cash flow basis, three of the companies were cheaper than it is. And on an EV to EBITDA basis, this number increased to four.

Company Price / Earnings Price / Operating Cash Flow EV / EBITDA
Champion Homes 29.9 20.2 14.8
M/I Homes (MHO) 5.8 24.1 4.4
Tri Pointe Homes (TPH) 7.0 5.7 5.2
KB Home (KBH) 6.6 12.9 6.7
Cavco Industries (CVCO) 25.5 27.2 16.3
Dream Finders Homes (DFH) 6.6 16.5 7.2

Takeaway

As things stand, I do think that Champion Homes is an interesting business and I fully expect it to continue to grow as time goes on. Unfortunately, shares look more expensive than I would like them to be, even if we assume that attractive growth continues as it has. When you add on top of this the risks facing the homebuilding industry, I believe that keeping the business rated a "hold" is the right choice.
